Patient is in the bed and asleep when she suddenly turns her head to the left - non versive; has left leg greater than arm automatisms. She opens here eyes and looks around. She places her left arm over the right hand and shuffles in the bed or moves her trunk with some bilateral leg movements. She was not tested for awareness during the episode.
She opens her eyes and looks around. She shuffles in the bed or moves her trunk with some bilateral leg movements. She was not tested for awareness during the episode.

She is sitting in bed and suddenly postures with her right hand flexed in a dystonic posture near her head while her left hand is on the bed. Then she flexes her right hand towards her body while her left hand extends and her eyes deviate to the left.
Her eyes are deviated to the left and she begins having clonic activity mainly with her left side while maintaining the dystonic posture of her right arm.
The patient becomes unresponsive to his mother and points with his left index finger with left arm external rotation.

The patient becomes unresponsive to his mother and points with his left index finger with left arm external rotation. This is then followed by high frequency movements of his head, flexion at the hips, and right head version.
The patient has high frequency movements of his head, flexion at the hips, and right head version. He then develops rhythmic jerking movements of all extremities.

The patient develops rhythmic jerking movements of all extremities with subsequent figure four position with right upper extremity extended or left upper extrimity flexed and right lower extrimity extended or left lower extrimity flexed. This is followed by tonic extension of all extremities.

The patient is in a figure four position with right upper extremity extended or left upper extrimity flexed and right lower extrimity extended or left lower extrimity flexed. This is followed by tonic extension of all extremities with subsequent clonic jerking movements.
The patient has tonic extension of all extremities with subsequent clonic jerking movements.

The patient is sitting in bed when he begins to exhibit bilateral arm external rotation movements and high frequency rhythmic head jerking movements.

He the exhibits tonic head deviation to the right with subsequent figure 4 position with right upper extremity extended and left upper extrimity flexed, also right lower extrimity extended and left lower extrimity flexed.
This is followed by tonic extension of all limbs with subsequent clonic jerking.
This is followed by tonic extension of all limbs with subsequent clonic jerking.

The patient exhibits very rapid right upper extremity finger movements with decreased verbal responsiveness. Rhythmic activity ceases briefly and is then followed first by head jerking then quickly also with left upper extrimity then bilateral symmetric arm external rotation jerks.

This is followed by head turn to the right with very high frequency movements; he then exhibits a figure four position of upper extremities with right upper extremity extension and left upper extrimity flexion prior to bilateral UE tonic extension and clonic movements.
The patient exhibits a figure four position of upper extremities with right upper extremity extension and left upper extrimity flexion prior to bilateral UE tonic extension and clonic movements.

One of her visitors reported that the patient appeared to have a 'blank stare' and suddenly jumps back to awareness. Her care giver reports possibly a typical event. During rounds, patient reported possibly a typical event.
Semiologically, this was marked by the patient staring for a few seconds blankly into space. He then reanimated and pushed the call button to report the staring episode.
He then reanimated and pushed the call button to report the staring episode.
The patient pushed the button to report a weird feeling of his head feeling compressed. He also reported headache.
The patient was noted to push the call button beforehand. He then developed irregular body trembling. Eyes were closed. This was accompanied by moaning throughout the episode.
The patient was noted to push the call button beforehand. He then developed irregular body trembling.
The patient has irregular body trembling. His eyes are closed, and this is accompanied by moaning.

During the nursing evaluation, the patient is able to follow instructions and speak. He is noted to reanimate.
The patient continues to follow instructions during the nursing evaluation. Afterwards, he is noted to reanimate and is able to speak.

During the event, the patient remains with eyes open and is otherwise motionless, with intermittent yawning.

The patient, after remaining motionless, suddenly shakes his head and presses the button to verbalize 'I just had a seizure'.
The patient has just verbalized 'I just had a seizure' and is now in the post-ictal phase, communicating with staff.
The patient is in the post-ictal phase, communicating with staff as a nurse enters the room to assess him.
In the post-ictal phase, the patient is assessed by a nurse and is able to communicate and correctly identify objects.
In the post-ictal phase, the patient continues to communicate with the nurse, describing his typical seizure symptoms.
In the post-ictal phase, the patient communicates with staff over an intercom and correctly recalls a memory word.
The patient remains calm in the post-ictal phase after successfully completing a memory test.
Patient awake, supine on bed, and eating before clinical onset. After chewing, he is noted to start staring with occasional chewing movements continuing.
He is noted to start staring and keeps staring ahead and breathing.
He keeps staring ahead and breathing.
He keeps staring ahead, and at one point wipes his mouth with a napkin. He subsequently continues staring and reaches for the call light, though does not activate it.
He continues staring. After a bit he abruptly and briefly shakes his head.
Patient lying supine in bed, awake. Noted to have some chewing movements. Then rhythmic tremulousness is noted in arms and torso.
The rhythmic tremulousness increases in frequency and amplitude. Patient says “Not now!” and then reaches for the call button.

The shaking increases in amplitude and becomes asymmetric with the patient arrhythmically cycling his right forearm against his abdomen and chest. He is responsive, says “I’m having seizure,” and then starts to cry “no, no, no”.
The cycling movement of his right hand abruptly ends. Following the event, the patient is noted to have slow breathing, appears tired, and is initially unresponsive to verbal questioning.
Following the event the patient is noted to have slow breathing and appears tired. He is initially unresponsive to verbal questioning but becomes responsive again after a short time.
The patient remains in a post-event state with slow breathing and appears tired. He is initially unresponsive to verbal questioning but becomes responsive again after a short time.
Patient awake, seated on chair before clinical onset. He was alone at room when the physician entered. He remained motionless and when initially addressed, he did not answered.
Afterwards, he shook head and was able to follow instructions and respond accurately to questions while he had intermittent left greater than right upper limbs shaking movements.
He was able to follow instructions and respond accurately to questions while he had intermittent left greater than right upper limbs shaking movements.

He was able to follow instructions and respond accurately to questions.

Left sided extremities are subsequently involved prior to offset.
The video begins with the patient already in a generalized seizure, with medical staff providing support. This corresponds to the phase after she "extends her right arm and generalizes."

She is sitting and starring at 11:20:32. She smiles and laughs at 11:20:43, followed by eyes version to right, then clonic arms followed by versive head and body to left by 11:20:54. She then extends her right arm and generalizes.
The patient's generalized seizure continues. A nurse prepares and administers rescue medication to end the seizure.
The patient continues in the generalized phase of the seizure.

The patient's dyscognitive language symptoms persist as the nurse continues to ask questions. He is unable to engage in a coherent conversation, confirming his confused state.

The patient has performed hyperventilation to trigger this event. Her right lower face has a sustained contraction, pulling her lip towards the right. She is able to follow simple commands and nod appropriately.
The patient's right lower face continues to have a sustained contraction, pulling her lip to the right. She is able to follow commands and repeats the words "apple pie" when asked.

The patient's right facial contraction persists. She is able to follow commands and perform simple calculations, correctly stating there are seven nickels in 35 cents after an initial incorrect answer.

With the right facial contraction still present, the patient is able to follow commands, clapping her hands and pointing to the ceiling. She states that this is not a normal feeling for her and that her heart is beating fast.
The patient's right facial contraction continues. She states her heart is beating fast and that her typical episodes last 2-3 minutes. She is able to repeat "Mary had a little lamb."

The patient's right lower face continues to have a sustained contraction with intermittent subtle twitching in her cheek. A nurse begins to take her vitals while she keeps her eyes closed as instructed.
The sustained contraction in her right lower face begins to gradually subside. The patient endorses a tired feeling in her legs.

The right facial contraction continues to gradually subside, though intermittent subtle twitching is still visible. The patient states that this is a more intense ("large") version of her typical event at home.
The right facial contraction continues to resolve. The patient is able to correctly state the date is July 1st. She denies that the symptoms ever spread to other parts of her body.

As the facial contraction continues to subside, the patient attempts to smile, showing residual right-sided facial weakness. She is able to hold her arms out straight and put her palms together when asked.
The event, which lasted about 5 minutes, has mostly resolved. The patient is able to rest her arms. The primary manifestation was the sustained right facial contraction.

The contraction has mostly subsided. The patient states she is not quite back to baseline as her face still feels tight on the right side, but it is loosening. She can smile, but some asymmetry is still visible.
The event has fully resolved, but the patient reports residual tightness and asymmetry in her right face, which is visible when she smiles.
